title: Chronic Bronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Vaccine Recommendations
content: The 21-valent pneumococcal conjugate (PCV21) alone, 20-valent PCV20 alone, or 15-valent PCV15 followed by 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) for all adults 19 and older with chronic lung disease. PCV21 is the preferred formulation in adult patients except those at increased risk of serotype 4. PCV20 provides coverage for serotype 4 and is the vaccine of choice for residents of the Navajo Nation or individuals living in the Western United States and Canada who have substance use disorder or are experiencing homelessness. Those who previously received only the PPSV23, PPV10, or PPV13 should receive PPV21 or PPV20 at least 1 year after receiving PPSV23. Healthcare professionals can access the most recent pneumococcal vaccine guidelines, including revaccination recommendations, at the United States Centers for Disease Control's website. [49]

title: Prevention Strategies -- Issues of Concern
content: Fragmented guidance also poses a challenge. Globally, the World Health Organization promotes prevention frameworks for vaccination, screening, and population health. In the United States, multiple organizations contribute to preventive care efforts. The US Preventive Services Task Force publishes evidence-based guidelines for primary and secondary prevention. In contrast, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention leads initiatives in chronic disease prevention, tobacco cessation, and immunization. However, the sheer number of recommending bodies and frequent updates to clinical guidelines can burden healthcare providers and make implementation difficult across healthcare systems.

title: Bullous Emphysema -- Treatment / Management -- Preventive Care
content: Annual influenza vaccine Pneumococcal vaccine based on local guidelines. In the United States and Canada, the current guidelines are the pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV) 21, except for residents of the Navajo Nation or individuals who reside in the Western United States and Canada who have substance use disorder or who experience homelessness. These patients should receive PCV20 or PCV15, followed by the pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) due to the lack of serotype 4 in PCV21. Further guidelines regarding recommendations for revaccinations and patients who have previously received PPSV23 are available through the United States Centers for Disease Control at Expanded Recommendations for Use of Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccines Among Adults Aged ≥50 Years: Recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices — United States, 2024 . title: Nicotine Addiction (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Nicotine Replacement Medications
content: Rimonabant: a cannabinoid receptor antagonist which is effective as an aid for smoking cessation.It is believed to contribute to reinforcing effects of nicotine action. Two other medications of interest: Mecamylamine: a noncompetitive antagonist of nicotinic cholinergic receptors; blocks nicotinic effect as well as reinforcement lobeline: an alkaloid which is a nicotine receptor agonist; satisfies the nicotine receptor. Phase 1 and phase 2 clinical trials are studying the effect of a nicotine vaccine that produces antibodies that bind to nicotine to prevent it from acting on nicotine receptors.

title: Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System -- Definition/Introduction
content: There are 3 parts to the survey questionnaire—core, optional, and state-added questions. All states use the core component questions. The annual core questions are asked yearly. Rotating core questions are 2 sets of questions used in alternating years. The emerging core consists of 5 questions that address current or emerging issues such as vaccine shortage and influenza-like illness. Optional modules include rotating core questions that states may elect to use. Each state selects state-added questions based on its specific needs. [5] [9] The questionnaire is designed to benefit individual states while also generating valuable national data. [2]

title: Immunization -- Indications
content: Childhood vaccinations are indicated for the prevention of a multitude of viral and bacterial infections and their sequelae. Specific vaccination recommendations for children vary by region, as exposure to diseases varies by region. Current recommendations for adult vaccination include a yearly influenza vaccine and tetanus-diphtheria every 10 years, with tetanus-diphtheria-acellular-pertussis at least once in adulthood. The addition of acellular pertussis once during adulthood is intended to prevent the transmission of whooping cough to young children or those unable to be vaccinated. For those adults born after 1956 who didn't receive measles, mumps, rubella vaccinations, or varicella vaccinations as children, these vaccinations are recommended in adulthood. Human papillomavirus, hepatitis A, and hepatitis B vaccines are also recommended in adulthood. The meningococcal vaccine is recommended for adults at high risk, such as those living in close quarters (college students living in dorms, military personnel living in barracks). Pneumococcal vaccination is also recommended for high-risk adults, such as those with pre-existing pulmonary diseases like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [8]

title: Asthma and COPD Overlap -- Treatment / Management
content: ACO management is mainly divided into two categories: nonpharmacological and pharmacological. Nonpharmacological measures include smoking cessation, which helps reduce the rate of  FEV1 decline. [19] Also, avoidance of exposure to other sources of smoke can be helpful. Since infections are one of the leading causes of exacerbation of asthma and COPD, getting the appropriate vaccines will help to reduce those exacerbations. This includes yearly influenza vaccine and pneumonia vaccines. Other interventions include education about proper inhaler technique and referral to pulmonary rehabilitation. [20]

title: Nicotine Replacement Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Objectives: Identify appropriate candidates for nicotine replacement therapy based on their smoking history, nicotine dependence, and willingness to quit. Differentiate between various forms of nicotine replacement therapy, such as patches, lozenges, inhalers, sprays, and gum, and understand their mechanisms of action. Implement personalized nicotine replacement therapy plans depending on the patient's smoking habits and preferences and provide clear instructions on proper usage, dosing, and duration. Communicate with patients effectively about nicotine replacement therapy benefits, possible adverse effects, and expected outcomes, ensuring they clearly understand its role in smoking cessation. title: Berylliosis  -- Treatment / Management
content: The goals when treating berylliosis are to reduce symptoms and slow the progression of the disease as no cure is available. Although there is no evidence that stopping exposure to beryllium decreases the progression of the disease, they still consider it to be an accepted approach to treatment. People with early stages, without lung function abnormalities or clinical symptoms, are periodically monitored, with physical exams, pulmonary function tests, and radiography. All patients require influenza and pneumococcal vaccination and smoking cessation counseling. After the appearance of clinical symptoms or significant abnormalities appears in pulmonary function testing, oxygen and oral corticosteroids are started as well as other supportive therapy as required. [1]

title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
content: The vaccine is a non-infectious subunit of the virus, which leads to an active immunity. Antibodies produced by vaccination target the outer protein coat or surface antigen. This protects against all virus genotypes (A through H) and gives a broad immunity. [4] It is generally accepted that the hepatitis B vaccine confers lifelong protection; originally, it was thought to only last around 8 years, but more recent research has confirmed ongoing immunity for at least 25 years in those individuals who demonstrated a good immune response from the vaccine series. [5] [6] Patients who fail to develop a good response from the vaccine tend to be over 40 years of age, obese, have celiac disease, smoke tobacco, or misuse alcohol. This last group is especially affected if they have progressed to liver disease. [7] [8]

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
content: The anti‑addiction/substance‑abuse treatment agents category includes alcohol deterrents/anti‑craving agents, with naltrexone hydrochloride classified under dependence on opiate compounds. Within this hierarchy, smoking‑cessation agents (e.g., bupropion) and opiate‑dependence agents are child categories, and the combination product bupropion/naltrexone illustrates naltrexone’s use across multiple addiction‑treatment modalities. Drugs used in alcohol dependence (e.g., disulfiram, which is contraindicated with paraldehyde) and drugs used in nicotine dependence (members of the vareniklin class) are subclasses of drugs used in addictive disorders; naltrexone is also identified as an alcohol‑cessation agent for treating alcohol dependence. In hepatic pathology, liver‑cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), including cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct, is clinically associated with liver inflammation (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) and with yellow liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is a risk factor for liver inflammation, and hepatitis, a specific form of liver inflammation, exemplifies the inflammatory damage that can result from chronic alcohol use. These interrelationships highlight the connections between addictive‑disorder pharmacotherapy, alcohol‑related hepatic injury, and malignant transformation of the liver.

Within this hierarchy, naltrexone hydrochloride is the parent compound for alcohol‑deterrent agents and is classified both as a drug used in alcohol dependence and as a heterocyclic compound; it is an inverse‑isa member of odvykací prostředky alkoholu. Disulfiram, another alcohol‑dependence medication, belongs to the same drug‑class group but is contraindicated when combined with paraldehyde, a heterocyclic agent that shares chemical ancestry with naltrexone and tulathromycin. In nicotine dependence, varenicline (varenin) is the primary pharmacologic agent and is also listed under Anti‑Addiction/Substance Abuse Treatment Agents. title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
content: Anti-Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents represent a broad therapeutic class that includes several specialized subcategories. Alcohol Deterrents/Anti-craving agents are classified as a child category within Anti-Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents, focusing on reducing the desire for alcohol and supporting abstinence. Likewise, látky pro odvykání kouření (substances for smoking cessation) are also a child category of Anti-Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents, designed to aid individuals in quitting tobacco use. Recognizing the contraindication of bupropion and the possible benefit of tofisopam is important for clinicians when selecting safe and effective pharmacologic strategies for individuals undergoing withdrawal.

content: Citalopram, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor used to treat depression and anxiety disorders, may have its therapeutic outcomes influenced by تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use or smoking). title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
content: The anti‑addiction/substance‑abuse treatment agents class includes alcohol deterrents/anti‑craving agents whose primary member is naltrexone hydrochloride. Naltrexone is used as an alcohol‑deterrent medication, appears in the bupropion/naltrexone combination, is listed among drugs used in alcohol dependence, and is classified as an alcohol withdrawal agent (odvykací prostředky alkoholu). Accordingly, naltrexone treats alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) and opioid dependence (الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية). Disulfiram is another member of the drugs used in alcohol dependence; it prevents alcohol dependence but has a contraindicated relationship with paraldehyde, which should be avoided when disulfiram is prescribed. In the nicotine‑dependence domain, varenicline (vareniklin) is a recognized therapeutic agent. title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving agents are classified under naltrexone hydrochloride, which itself is placed within the broader category of opioid‑dependence treatments. These agents belong to the larger group of Anti‑Addiction/Substance‑Abuse Treatment Agents, a class that is closely linked to drugs used in addictive disorders. The umbrella of drugs used in addictive disorders encompasses three major subclasses: drugs used in alcohol dependence, drugs used in nicotine dependence, and drugs used in opioid dependence. Within the alcohol‑dependence subclass, disulfiram is a recognized member; it is contraindicated when combined with the class of paraldehyde compounds. The nicotine‑dependence subclass includes vareniklin as a representative agent. The opioid‑dependence subclass comprises several agents, notably buprenorphine, levomethadone, and lofexidine. Buprenorphine is identified as a naturally occurring alkaloid and a plant‑derived product, and it is specifically associated with maintenance therapy for opioid use (“علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية”). Lofexidine, together with beta‑funaltrexamine, is categorized as a narcotics antagonist. The maintenance therapy for opioid substances is considered part of the broader drug‑withdrawal syndrome grouping (SMQ), reflecting its role in managing withdrawal symptoms. Collectively, these hierarchical relationships illustrate how specific pharmacologic agents are organized within larger therapeutic classes, how they relate to particular substance‑use disorders, and how contraindications and mechanistic classifications (e.g., alkaloid origin, antagonist activity) inform clinical decision‑making and patient care.

This hierarchy indicates that naltrexone hydrochloride is a medication primarily associated with the management of opioid dependence, and that formulations used to deter alcohol consumption or reduce craving are considered specific applications of this drug. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the shared pharmacologic basis between treatments for alcohol craving and opioid dependence, supporting more integrated and informed approaches to managing substance‑use disorders.
